metadata.summary: Dataset lifecycle: cleaning, deduplication, format conversion (Alpaca/ShareGPT/OpenAI), hyperparameter selection, evaluation benchmarks, and deployment to Ollama/vLLM/TGI.

content.title: Fine-tuning Datasets — Preparation, Training, and Deployment
content.summary: Dataset lifecycle methodology for open-model fine-tuning: cleaning and deduplication, format conversion (Alpaca/ShareGPT/OpenAI JSONL), training hyperparameter selection, evaluation with LM Evaluation Harness benchmarks, and deployment to Ollama (GGUF), vLLM, or TGI. Includes GPU selection and cost estimation for training runs.

why.p: Dataset format consistency is the single highest-impact factor in fine-tuning quality. Semantic duplicates, inconsistent system prompts, and unsanitized PII silently degrade model behavior. Getting the cleaning pipeline right before training saves GPU spend and avoids retraining cycles. Export format must match the inference server (GGUF for Ollama, merged HF for vLLM/TGI).

list.item: Preparing a domain-specific dataset for LoRA/QLoRA or OpenAI fine-tuning from raw source data
Evaluating a fine-tuned model against baselines using standard benchmarks (MMLU, HellaSwag, HumanEval)
Deploying a fine-tuned model to a local (Ollama) or production (vLLM/TGI) inference server
Estimating GPU cost and training duration before committing cloud spend
Fewer than 100 cleaned, verified examples — dataset preparation overhead is not justified; use few-shot instead
Benchmark accuracy on MMLU/HellaSwag is the primary goal — task-specific fine-tuning often hurts general benchmarks
Deployment target does not support GGUF or HF-format models
vLLM deployment without first merging the LoRA adapter — vLLM requires a merged model

rule.statement: Deduplicate by (instruction, output) exact hash; use semantic deduplication tools for datasets where near-duplicates are likely.
rule.rationale: Exact deduplication misses semantic duplicates; a model trained on near-duplicate data overfits silently while appearing to have sufficient coverage.

rule.statement: Remove PII (email addresses, phone numbers, full names) from training data before upload to any external provider.
rule.rationale: Training data is processed on provider servers and may influence model outputs; PII in training data is a data governance and compliance risk.

rule.statement: Tune output length filters per task: do not use a global min/max. Short-answer tasks (yes/no, single-word) are invalid short outputs for most tasks but valid for others.
rule.rationale: A global min-length filter of 10 characters removes valid single-word answers in classification tasks; filter thresholds must be task-specific.

rule.statement: Format consistency is the highest-impact factor — use the same system prompt across all examples, or omit it entirely from all examples.
rule.rationale: A fine-tuned model that saw mixed system-prompt/no-system-prompt training behaves inconsistently at inference when one or the other is provided.

list.item: Alpaca: {"instruction": "...", "input": "...", "output": "..."} — for single-turn tasks
ShareGPT: {"conversations": [{"from": "human", "value": "..."}, {"from": "gpt", "value": "..."}]} — for multi-turn chat
OpenAI JSONL: {"messages": [{"role": "system"}, {"role": "user"}, {"role": "assistant"}]} — for OpenAI API fine-tuning

rule.statement: Split with a fixed random seed (random.seed(42)) after shuffling — never split sequentially, as it leaks temporal distribution bias.
rule.rationale: Sequential split means the validation set is always the "oldest" data; shuffle-and-split produces a representative val set across the full data distribution.

rule.statement: Use 80-90% for training, 10-20% for validation; keep a separate held-out test set (5-10%) that is never used for training or hyperparameter tuning.
rule.rationale: Evaluating on validation data used for early stopping leaks information; the held-out test set is the only unbiased quality signal.

rule.statement: Start with learning_rate=2e-4, batch_size=2, gradient_accumulation=4, epochs=3, cosine scheduler — adjust only after observing validation loss curves.
rule.rationale: These defaults produce stable training on 7B models with LoRA. Starting with defaults and adjusting from observed loss behavior is faster than grid-searching from scratch.

list.item: learning_rate: 1e-5 to 5e-4 for LoRA; lower (1e-6 to 5e-6) for DPO/ORPO
batch_size: 1-8 per GPU, effective batch = batch * grad_accum * n_gpus
epochs: 1-5; 3 is a safe default; increase if training loss is still decreasing at epoch 3
warmup_ratio: 0.03-0.1 — prevents loss spikes in early training
weight_decay: 0.01-0.1 — light regularization; higher for overfitting risk
max_seq_length: 512-8192 — set to 95th percentile of dataset sequence length to avoid padding waste

rule.statement: Use bfloat16 on A100/H100 instead of float16 — avoids NaN loss without quality loss; use float16 only on older GPU architectures that do not support bf16.
rule.rationale: bfloat16 has a wider exponent range than float16, preventing gradient underflow/overflow that causes NaN loss on long training runs.

list.item: 7B full FT: 28GB VRAM → A100 40GB ($3.50/hr)
13B LoRA: 28GB VRAM → A100 40GB ($3.50/hr)
70B QLoRA: 40-48GB VRAM → A100 80GB ($4.50/hr)
70B full FT: 280GB VRAM → 4x A100 80GB ($18/hr)

rule.statement: Effective batch size = per_device_batch * gradient_accumulation * num_gpus; target effective batch 8-32 for stable training.
rule.rationale: Too small effective batch causes noisy gradients; too large reduces generalization. Gradient accumulation achieves large effective batch on limited VRAM.

rule.statement: Run lm-eval on MMLU before and after fine-tuning to detect catastrophic forgetting early — a drop of more than 5 points on MMLU indicates the model has lost general capabilities.
rule.rationale: Fine-tuning on narrow task data reduces general benchmark performance; tracking MMLU delta quantifies the forgetting cost and gates whether the specialized gain is worth it.

rule.statement: Never use perplexity on training data as an eval metric — always compute on held-out data from the same distribution.
rule.rationale: Training perplexity approaches 0 as the model memorizes the training set; it is not a quality signal. Only held-out perplexity measures generalization.

rule.statement: lm-eval benchmarks take 2-8 hours on a 7B model — dispatch async and await completion signal; do not poll in a tight loop.
rule.rationale: Tight polling wastes CPU and produces no value during long-running evaluation; use file-based completion signals or webhook notifications.

rule.statement: Use q4_k_m quantization for Ollama deployment — good balance of quality and file size; use q8_0 only if perplexity difference is measurable on your eval set.
rule.rationale: q4_k_m is the community standard for local serving; q8_0 doubles file size with marginal quality improvement for most tasks.

example.code: # Unsloth: export to GGUF directly from training model.save_pretrained_gguf("model_gguf", tokenizer, quantization_method="q4_k_m")
example.note: Unsloth handles the merge-and-quantize pipeline in one step.

rule.statement: Always write GGUF files to a persistent volume path — never to /tmp on a cloud instance that terminates after training.
rule.rationale: Cloud GPU instances (RunPod, Lambda) terminate and lose /tmp on shutdown; writing to /tmp produces a lost model after an expensive training run.

rule.statement: Merge LoRA adapter before deploying to vLLM — pass the merged model directory, not the PEFT adapter path.
rule.rationale: vLLM requires a standard HF model; providing a PEFT adapter path causes a silent hang or error at startup.

rule.statement: Set --tensor-parallel-size to match available GPU count exactly; a mismatch causes a silent hang at vLLM startup.
rule.rationale: vLLM uses NCCL for tensor parallelism; wrong GPU count causes an NCCL init hang that appears as a startup freeze with no error message.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Dataset preparation is well-suited for agent automation: an agent reads raw source data, applies cleaning rules, formats to the target schema (Alpaca/ShareGPT/OpenAI), deduplicates, and writes validated JSONL. Deployment steps (GGUF export, Ollama model creation, vLLM server launch) are scripted commands that an agent can orchestrate via shell calls. Human checkpoints are required before: (1) uploading training data to a provider, (2) running evaluation benchmarks, (3) serving the model in production.

list.item: faion-sdd-execution — automate dataset cleaning pipeline: read raw data → deduplicate → filter → format → validate → write JSONL
Custom eval agent — run lm-eval after training and produce a comparison table vs. base model

example.code: Clean the following dataset. Remove duplicates (by instruction+output pair), filter outputs shorter than 20 chars or longer than 2000 chars, and remove any item containing PII (email addresses, phone numbers, full names). Return cleaned JSON array. Input: {raw_json}

list.item: Deduplication by exact (instruction, output) key pair misses semantic duplicates — a model trained on near-duplicate data overfits silently
`len(output) < 10` filters remove valid short-answer examples (yes/no, single-word answers) — tune thresholds per task
Validation split must be random-stratified; sequential splitting leaks temporal distribution bias
vLLM requires a merged model (not a PEFT adapter) — agents that skip the merge step produce a broken deployment
GPU cost estimates from estimate_training_time() assume constant throughput; real throughput varies ±30% by sequence length distribution
TGI Docker image versions lag behind HF model releases — new model families may require a specific image tag
Dataset generation by LLM introduces systematic bias (same vocabulary, similar sentence structures) — validate with diversity metrics (unique bigram ratio)
An agent writing GGUF files to /tmp on a cloud instance will lose them on pod termination — always specify a persistent volume path
vLLM `--tensor-parallel-size` must match available GPU count exactly; wrong value silently hangs at startup
`lm-eval` benchmarks can take 2-8 hours on a 7B model — agents must dispatch async and await completion signal, not poll in a tight loop
Perplexity on the training set is not a valid eval metric — always compute on held-out data from the same distribution
Minimum viable dataset: 100 examples for narrow task, 500+ for general instruction following, 1000+ for style/persona transfer
Quality > quantity: 200 expertly curated examples outperform 2000 noisy ones consistently
Format consistency is the single highest-impact factor — use the same system prompt across all examples or omit it entirely
For ShareGPT multi-turn data: include at least 3 turns per conversation; single-turn ShareGPT is equivalent to Alpaca
Use q4_k_m GGUF for Ollama (good balance); q8_0 only if perplexity difference is measurable on your eval set
Run lm_eval on MMLU before and after fine-tuning to detect catastrophic forgetting early
TGI requires `--max-input-length` and `--max-total-tokens` tuned to your data distribution — default values cause OOM on long contexts
lm-eval: Run standard benchmarks (MMLU, HellaSwag, HumanEval). Install: pip install lm-eval · github.com/EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness
datasets: HF library for loading, processing, splitting datasets. Install: pip install datasets · huggingface.co/docs/datasets
ollama: Local model serving from GGUF. Install: curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh · ollama.ai
vllm: Production OpenAI-compatible inference server. Install: pip install vllm · docs.vllm.ai
docker: Run TGI inference container. Website: docker.com · Image: ghcr.io/huggingface/text-generation-inference
unsloth: Export to GGUF directly from training. Install: pip install unsloth · github.com/unslothai/unsloth
Hugging Face Hub (SaaS/OSS): Dataset hosting, model registry, AutoTrain. Agent-friendly via SDK + REST API.
Weights & Biases (SaaS): Experiment tracking, loss curves, eval scores. Agent-friendly via REST API.
RunPod (SaaS): GPU rental for training/inference; no managed eval. Partial agent support.
Lambda Labs (SaaS): Cheaper than AWS for A100/H100; SSH access only. Partial agent support.
Vast.ai (SaaS): Auction-based GPU; cheapest but variable reliability. Partial agent support via REST API.
Ollama (OSS): Local serving; agent can call `/api/generate`. Agent-friendly via REST API on :11434.
vLLM (OSS): Production serving; best throughput for batched inference. Agent-friendly via OpenAI-compatible API.

section.name: Train/Val Split Code
section.p: Split dataset into train/val with stratification proxy (shuffle + ratio):

example.code:
import json, random
def split_dataset(path: str, val_ratio: float = 0.1, seed: int = 42) -> tuple: with open(path) as f: data = json.load(f) random.seed(seed) random.shuffle(data) split = int(len(data) * val_ratio) return data[split:], data[:split] # train, val
train, val = split_dataset("cleaned.json", val_ratio=0.1) with open("train.json", "w") as f: json.dump(train, f) with open("val.json", "w") as f: json.dump(val, f) print(f"Train: {len(train)}, Val: {len(val)}")

template.purpose: Ollama Modelfile for LLaMA-3 chat template with stop tokens
